item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why would somebody propose using 1407-infected people to contain 1009? Isn't it much more burdensome to expend and care for D-class personnel than it is to maintain speakers? Isn't it an additional burden to also do 1407 containment at the 1009 site?

For that matter, are you nuts?! You're going to keep 1407 breeding in sombre outdoor location? That's not even risking containment breach, that's actively facilitating it.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image used in this article is cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as it was taken by the author for the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Terrifying.

Came to this by crosslink.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good body horror, I may have nightmares tonight…
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Weak concept and a bunch of run-on sentences.
-1

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: I like it.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Stopped. Edits made.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ show block
– hide block

I propose the following addition:

Addendum: A proposal has been requested by Dr. █████ to use D-class personnel affected by SCP-1407 to circle around SCP-1009 so that only one speaker needs to be maintained. On-site medical staff are to be present to care for any injuries caused by SCP-1407.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Currently, 11 instances of SCP-1407 are in containment.

This implies that there are more out there that haven't been discovered, so Euclid would be fair here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would upvote but this is not an euclid. It's safe. If you want people to guess that there may be other instances of the scip out there, you should say it. As it is, it's safe.

Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the sort of object that should be shown to people first joining the site. Short, scary, and original.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This just, to me, seems like a 'thing what does what it would normally do, but in an over the top/paranormal way'
I want to like it, but as it is, it seems to be squick for the sake of squick.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked the level of weirdness in this!!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A distinctly retro feel. Simple, short, squick-ilicious; and up-voted with enthusiasm.

One missing detail. How long before the wires reach the cochlea? Is the safe-to-wear time an hour, a few minutes, a few seconds?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you :D. Anyway, I really like this skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am so, so glad i was in the chat for this one…

+1, not because i helped gestate this thing, but because body horror is like jello: there is always room for more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is fairly simple, but the squick factor and general freakishness (I hate earbuds) are quite effective. I upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There was a sharp intake of breath halfway through the article. Wonderfully unsettling, good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That was the best summing up of when to use redaction that I have ever seen.

E. As for the article, I really like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man, this is not a good advice. This is the kind of articles where the person who is reading it needs to know as much as possible about the skip's behavior. Also, [DATA EXPUNGED] only works well with skips that people doesn't need (or need to avoid) any kind of sensitive knowledge that would breach containment if it's spreaded.

Hi, I'm agent Deep, and I need to know how does exactly a parasitoid earbuds work.

Hi, I‘m agent Sanders, and I think you don’t need to know about [DATA EXPUNGED] because then it grows and starts to kill everything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this could still work as a Factory item if it were tweaked somewhat. Perhaps they're high-quality audiophile earbuds that genuinely create the best musical fidelity possible, but they begin to incorporate themselves more into the body with each use and not using them causes worse and worse withdrawl complications until the user basically has to either rock out 24/7 or die?

Just a thought.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I see where y'all are coming from. I'll remove the link.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seconding Mac here. Factory items always have some aspect that make you WANT to use them. They do something really cool, that either can be twisted by the user (like the monkeys) or exacts some price (like the porn.)
I would upvote this if it weren't a Factory item.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have the same issue; Factory products are, well, products. I don't recall a single one being what is effectively a self-replicating parasitoid that invariably kills its host.

Edit: I would be perfectly fine with this if it did not have the Factory link. As it is, I have to downvote it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll likely up vote, but a data expunged in the effects in one's body would force the reader to use their imagination a little bit. Still, it's pretty good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What about it doesn't seem like factory?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Down voted.

It's a nice standalone piece, but it really doesn't seem like a factory item.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Only upvoting for the end bit about vibrating all up inside you with sound 'till you blow up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty classic-feeling and squicky. I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Hey, just let the wires do their thing! They're not hassling you, man. This isAmerica! The land of the eagle!

…Alright, seriously, you should have not bought this. You're just stupid."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is put together well enough, but it doesn't really do much for me. No vote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this, although as mentioned on IRC I think the virus/self-replication angle has been done quite a bit before. It plays well on squeamishness associated with earbud use - I think the decision to move away from pure eye scream to more general body horror was a good one and it leaves enough questions in the user's mind about the purpose of the earbuds. In particular, I note the dB threshold means the secondary effect will mostly manifest when the user is in a crowded area with lots of other people who might pick up a pair of earbuds skittering over the floor…
